heroTitle: Build your own Tamil sentences with core verbs.
heroText: This lesson shifts from memorizing phrases to using high-value spoken patterns. The goal is to help learners start making their own short Tamil sentences.
panelList: How to use a few core spoken verbs over and over
How to build short sentences with I, want, do, go, come, and understand
How to recognize sentence patterns instead of memorizing isolated lines
title: Core verbs and sentence patterns
description: This lesson introduces the verbs and structures that let a beginner build their own spoken Tamil. Instead of learning only fixed lines, the learner starts seeing patterns like 'I am going', 'I want', 'I don't understand', and 'I am coming'.

audio.note: This localhost lesson is wired for ElevenLabs-generated audio. Configure ELEVENLABS_API_KEY and ELEVENLABS_VOICE_ID in .env.local, then run serve.py.

phrases.useCase: A useful pattern for saying you are doing something right now.
phrases.note: This teaches the beginner a very reusable present-action structure.

phrases.useCase: Use this to say where you are going.
phrases.note: This helps the learner see how location plus `poren` works.

phrases.useCase: A very common home reply when someone calls you.
phrases.note: This is one of the highest-value short response patterns in the app.

phrases.useCase: A useful beginner pattern for saying what you want or need.
phrases.note: `enakku ... venum` is one of the most useful sentence frames in spoken Tamil.

phrases.useCase: Useful when someone wants an answer or action immediately.
phrases.note: This helps learners see how the same `venum` pattern works with many nouns.

phrases.useCase: A survival phrase and also an important sentence pattern.
phrases.note: This is different from `puriyala`, which is more like 'I didn't understand.'

phrases.useCase: Use this when you heard the words but did not understand the meaning.
phrases.note: This is a core learner phrase and a strong sentence-building pattern.

phrases.useCase: Useful for learning how action verbs change in spoken Tamil.
phrases.note: This helps learners hear a natural spoken form rather than a stiff textbook form.

phrases.useCase: Use this when you want to say your current state without giving a long explanation.
phrases.note: This gives the learner a very practical `irukken` structure.

phrases.useCase: Useful for everyday home situations when someone is waiting on you.
phrases.note: This is a nice bridge from fixed phrases into flexible self-statements.

phrases.useCase: Useful when someone asks what you are doing or you want to describe your action.
phrases.note: This reinforces how `pannren` can attach to many action ideas in spoken Tamil.

phrases.useCase: Use when someone asks you something and you are about to answer.
phrases.note: A useful bridge phrase that helps the learner keep talking.

dialogues.prompt: You want to say you are doing something right now.
dialogues.context: Which line gives the clearest everyday present-action pattern?
dialogues.explanation: This is the simplest reusable present-action pattern in the lesson.

dialogues.context: Which answer best shows the `venum` pattern?
dialogues.explanation: This is the strongest example of the beginner-friendly `enakku ... venum` structure.

dialogues.prompt: You heard the Tamil words, but you still do not know what they mean.
dialogues.explanation: Use `puriyala` for not understanding meaning; `theriyala` is closer to not knowing.

dialogues.prompt: A family member is waiting and you want to say you're coming now.
dialogues.explanation: This is one of the most common short-response patterns in spoken home Tamil.

recalls.note: A core pattern for present action.
